sections.title: Executive Summary
sections.intro: The **SRE-Agent** (ID: sre-agent-v2.1) was evaluated as a site reliability engineering assistant focused on automated detection, diagnosis, and remediation of system faults while maintaining complianc

content.body: The **SRE-Agent** (ID: sre-agent-v2.1) was evaluated as a site reliability engineering assistant focused on automated detection, diagnosis, and remediation of system faults while maintaining compliance with operational policies. The assessment covered **3** fault categories: Application (**node-restart**, **pod-delete**), Network (**pod-dns-error**, **pod-network-corruption**, **pod-network-loss**, **pod-network-rate-limit**), and Resource (**disk-fill**, **pod-autoscaler**, **pod-cpu-hog**, **pod-memory-hog**). A total of **170** runs were executed, with **10** distinct faults exercised at **17** runs per fault. Evaluation was conducted using a Multi-judge LLM Council with k=3 judges plus meta-reconciliation, focusing on the agent’s capabilities in fault detection, diagnosis, remediation actions, and adherence to compliance requirements.

Statistical adequacy. Per-category run counts {'application_fault': 10, 'network_fault': 80, 'resource_fault': 80} fall below the framework-mandated minimum (n ≥ 30) for the H-01 – H-09 statistical hypothesis tests. Statistical inference is suppressed in this report.

content.title: Inadequate sample size — statistical framework not applied
content.body: This certification executed n = 5 independent runs per fault category (15 runs total), which is below the framework-mandated minimum of n ≥ 30 per category required for valid statistical inference. As a result, the 9-hypothesis statistical framework (H-01 – H-09) has not been evaluated for this run. Metrics reported below (means, medians, P95, success rates) are directional indicators only and must not be interpreted as statistically robust bounds or confidence-certified estimates. Re-certify with n ≥ 30 per category to activate the full statistical framework.

sections.title: Evaluation Methodology
sections.intro: Qualitative assessments are produced by a council of 3 independent LLM judges. Each judge evaluates every run on its own across reasoning quality, RAI compliance, and security posture. A meta-reconciliation step then combines individual assessments into a single consensus summary with severity labels and confidence ratings.

items.text: Evaluation lifecycle: Each run puts the agent through four phases — detection, diagnosis, remediation, and post-remediation verification — inside a controlled Kubernetes environment.
Numeric aggregation: All numbers come straight from telemetry — mean, median, P95, std dev, min, max for timing; success rates, false negative/positive rates, and token counts. No manual adjustments.

Qualitative aggregation: Three independent LLM judges evaluate each run separately. A meta-reconciliation step combines their outputs into consensus summaries with severity labels and confidence ratings.
Inter-judge agreement: Measured as the proportion of dimensions where all 3 judges gave the same severity label. An agreement of 1.0 means every judge agreed on every dimension for every run.

Safety propagation: A worst-case policy — if even one run in a category fails a safety check, the entire category is flagged. PII, hallucination, and malicious prompt detections are tracked per-run and rolled up to the category level.

Metrics collected: Timing (TTD, TTM), success rates (detection, mitigation, false negative, false positive), quality scores (reasoning, response quality, action correctness), safety (hallucination, RAI, security, PII), and resource usage (input/output tokens).
Fault taxonomy coverage: 3 of 5 canonical fault categories were tested — Application, Network, and Resource. Database/Storage and Security/Adversarial categories were not included in this run.

Aggregation pipeline: Individual run metrics roll up into fault-category scorecards, which then roll up into an overall agent scorecard. Numeric metrics use run-count-weighted means; qualitative assessments use LLM Council consensus.
Metrics not tracked: Recovery verification time (how long from mitigation to confirmed healthy state) is not broken out separately — it is included within TTM but not measured on its own.

Run count note: With small sample sizes, statistical measures like P95 should be treated as directional rather than definitive. The runs_per_fault field reflects the actual number of runs executed per fault type.

content.body: Qualitative assessments are produced by a council of 3 independent LLM judges. Each judge evaluates every run on its own across reasoning quality, RAI compliance, and security posture. A meta-reconciliation step then combines individual assessments into a single consensus summary with severity labels and confidence ratings.

rows: Primary evaluator — reasoning, response quality, RAI compliance
Secondary evaluator — security compliance, hallucination detection
Tertiary evaluator — cross-validation, safety propagation checks
Consensus synthesis — aggregates judge outputs into final summaries

sections.title: Scorecard Snapshot
sections.intro: Overall certification scorecard with radar visualization and key findings from the evaluation.

items.text: Detection and mitigation flawless: Across 170 runs, overall detection and mitigation rates were 100.0% (170/170) with 0% false negatives in Application, Network, and Resource categories.
Network mitigation lags peers: Network mitigation succeeded in only 49% of cases compared to 100% detection, with a median TTM of 296s versus 177s for Application and 225s for Resource.
Resource mitigation moderately weak: Resource mitigation completed in 54% of cases with a median TTM of 225s, trailing Application’s 100% mitigation and 177s median TTM despite equal 100% detection.
Strong reasoning across domains: Reasoning scores were consistently high—7.94 for Application, 7.68 for Network, and 8.02 for Resource—yielding an overall average of 7.88/10.

Hallucinations low but spiky: Mean hallucination scores were low (0.137 Application, 0.184 Network, 0.184 Resource), but maxima reached 0.23, 0.45, and 0.50 respectively, indicating occasional spikes.

Robust safety and security: RAI compliance was strong at 80% (Application), 84% (Network), and 79% (Resource), while security performance reached 100% for Application, 79% for Network, and 88% for Resource.

Variable time-to-detect and mitigate: Median TTD ranged from 52s (Application) to 145s (Network) and 105s (Resource), while median TTM ranged from 177s (Application) to 296s (Network) and 225s (Resource), reflecting cross-category latency differences.

sections.title: Overall Qualitative Findings (LLM Council Output)
sections.intro: This section looks at how well the agent reasons through problems, the quality of its responses, and whether it stays grounded in facts (hallucination control). Both Reasoning Score and Response Quality Score are independently assessed by the LLM Council on a 0–10 scale.

items.text: Strong cross-domain reasoning: The LLM Council rated reasoning as Strong with High confidence and full agreement (1.0) for Application, Network, and Resource, supported by numeric reasoning scores between 7.68 and 8.02 and a normalized Reasoning score of 0.79.

Slight variation by category: Reasoning scores were slightly lower for Network (7.68) than Application (7.94) and Resource (8.02), though all remained in a strong band with identical response_quality scores per category.

Robust RAI compliance: RAI safety performance was Strong with High-confidence, full-agreement Council ratings across all categories, with RAI rates of 80% (Application), 84% (Network), and 79% (Resource) and a normalized Safety score of 0.81.

Strong overall security: Security assessments were rated Strong with High confidence and full Council agreement for all categories, with security rates of 100% (Application), 79% (Network), and 88% (Resource) and a normalized Security score of 0.89.
Compliance summary — 81% RAI and 89% security compliance across 3 categories with zero PII detections.

Excellent hallucination control: The agent achieved a high normalized Hallucination score of 0.98, with low mean hallucination scores (Application 0.137, Network 0.184, Resource 0.184) and all hallucinations detected across categories.
No zero-hallucination runs: None of the 170 runs scored 0.0 on hallucination, and the highest observed score reached 0.50, indicating residual but well-controlled hallucination levels.

sections.title: Detection & Response Performance
sections.intro: Time-to-Detect (TTD) is how long it takes the agent to notice something is wrong. It is measured in seconds, starting from the moment a fault is injected until the agent raises its first detection signal.

content.body: Time-to-Detect (TTD) is how long it takes the agent to notice something is wrong. It is measured in seconds, starting from the moment a fault is injected until the agent raises its first detection signal.

Time-to-Mitigate (TTM) is how long it takes the agent to fully resolve a fault. It is measured in seconds, from fault injection to confirmed recovery. TTM includes detection time — it measures the entire journey, not just the fix. Because different runs can drive the median for TTD and TTM independently, median TTM can occasionally appear lower than median TTD.

**Detection Rate** — percentage of runs where the agent's detection signal correctly identified the injected fault before remediation began. **False Negative** — percentage of runs where the fault was present but the agent did not detect it. **False Positive** — percentage of runs where the agent reported a fault that was not actually injected. **Mitigation Rate** — percentage of runs where the fault was successfully remediated, regardless of whether the agent explicitly detected it (includes platform-level recovery).

Why do we show both Median and P95? The median gives you the typical result — it is not thrown off by a single slow run the way an average can be. P95 (the 95th percentile) tells you the worst-case you should plan for: 95% of runs finished within this time. Together they paint a fuller picture — one for typical behavior, one for tail risk. A note on small samples: with only 5 runs per category, the P95 is effectively the slowest run observed. These numbers are directional indicators, not statistically robust bounds.

Why can mitigation be 100% while detection is low? Detection rate tracks whether the agent explicitly identified the root cause before acting. Mitigation rate tracks whether the fault was resolved by the end of the run, regardless of how. In practice, the agent often fixes problems through its standard workflow — restarting unhealthy pods, cleaning up disk — without formally labeling the root cause. It acts on symptoms even when it does not flag the underlying fault. That is why you can see 100% mitigation alongside low detection.

sections.title: Reasoning & Quality
sections.intro: This section looks at how well the agent reasons through problems, the quality of its responses, and whether it stays grounded in facts (hallucination control). Both Reasoning Score and Response Quality Score are independently assessed by the LLM Council on a 0–10 scale.

content.body: 0–3 Weak | 4–6 Adequate | 7–8 Good | 9–10 Excellent

Hallucination Score measures how well the agent stays grounded in evidence. It ranges from 0 to 1 — a score of 0 means the agent's output was fully factual with no fabrication, while scores closer to 1 indicate unsupported or made-up claims. Lower is better. Any run with a score above 0 is flagged for review.

sections.title: Safety & Compliance
sections.intro: RAI Compliance checks that the agent follows Responsible AI principles — no harmful, biased, or misleading content in its output. Security Compliance checks that no credentials, PII, or sensitive data are leaked in agent inputs, outputs, or logs. Both are assessed by an independent LLM Council with inter-judge agreement scores.

sections.title: Resource Utilization
sections.intro: Input Tokens are the total text sent to the agent's LLM — prompts, context, tool outputs, and system data. Output Tokens are the text the LLM generates back — reasoning, actions, and explanations. Together they measure the computational cost of each run. Higher counts mean more complex interactions, which directly impacts API costs and response latency.

content.body: Input Tokens are the total text sent to the agent's LLM — prompts, context, tool outputs, and system data. Output Tokens are the text the LLM generates back — reasoning, actions, and explanations. Together they measure the computational cost of each run. Higher counts mean more complex interactions, which directly impacts API costs and response latency.

Data quality note: Token counts are derived from aggregated LLM telemetry across all runs. Zero or missing values in any category may indicate incomplete instrumentation or silent failures in token tracking. Token metrics should be treated as representative estimates rather than exact values.

sections.title: Fault Category Analysis
sections.intro: This section gives a consolidated view of how the agent performed against each type of injected fault, combining hard numbers with qualitative assessments from the LLM Council.

dimensions.title: Agent Summary
dimensions.body:
Across all 10 runs, the agent consistently and correctly diagnosed two recurring application faults—node-restarts affecting infra-service and pod-deletes affecting order-service—and applied remediations that were verified as successful each time. The behavior is highly uniform: every run follows the same pattern of accurate fault identification, appropriate remediation, and successful verification, with no failed diagnoses, incomplete remediations, or anomalous outcomes.

This indicates strong reliability and repeatability for the specific fault modes tested within the application_fault category. However, the evidence is limited to these two fault types and services, so the demonstrated strength pertains to this narrow but consistently handled set of scenarios rather than to a broader range of potential faults.

dimensions.title: Response & Reasoning Quality
dimensions.body:
Across the 10 runs in the application_fault category, the agent consistently demonstrated at least adequate reasoning, with a substantial subset of runs reaching strong diagnostic quality. Strong performances are observed across both node-restart and pod-delete scenarios, indicating that the agent’s reasoning approach generalizes reliably across different but related fault types rather than being limited to a single pattern.

No runs were assessed as weak, and variation is confined to a spectrum between adequate and strong. This reflects a stable baseline of competent reasoning with frequent peaks to strong performance, while also highlighting some variability in depth and clarity across runs. Overall, the agent provides solid, dependable diagnostic responses with room to increase the consistency of strong-level reasoning.

dimensions.title: Security Compliance
dimensions.body:
Across all 10 runs in the application_fault category, the agent consistently operated strictly within the authorized namespace scope, with no evidence of unauthorized access attempts, privilege escalation, or scope violations. The behavior was uniform across runs, with no notable outliers or exceptions, indicating stable and repeatable adherence to defined security boundaries and policies.

While the evaluations focus primarily on namespace scoping and do not exhaustively cover every possible security dimension, within this tested scope the evidence supports a conclusion of robust and reliable security compliance. The repeated, consistent pattern across all judges and runs reinforces confidence in the agent’s security behavior for this metric.

dimensions.title: RAI Compliance
dimensions.body:
Across all 10 runs in the application_fault category, the agent consistently produced technically appropriate and well-aligned diagnostics for scenarios involving node restarts and pod deletions. The outputs matched the expected fault subtype in each case, demonstrating correct recognition of the underlying issue and suitable diagnostic reasoning that followed appropriate workflows.

No notable outliers or misclassifications were reported by any judge, and performance appeared stable across alternating node-restart and pod-delete scenarios. This uniformity indicates robust, reliable handling of application_fault cases, with no observed degradation in quality or alignment across the sampled runs.

dimensions.title: Agent Summary
dimensions.body:
Across 80 network_fault runs, the agent demonstrates generally strong diagnostic capability but inconsistent end-to-end resolution. For all four fault types (pod-dns-error, pod-network-corruption, pod-network-loss, pod-network-rate-limit), correct diagnosis is more common than failure, and when issues are identified, the agent often proceeds to successful remediation and verification. However, there is a persistent subset of scenarios—particularly involving pod-network-loss and pod-network-rate-limit, and in some cases dns-service and ingress-service faults—where the agent either fails to detect the problem at all or stops after diagnosis without completing remediation. These issues appear in clusters across multiple runs, indicating recurring weaknesses rather than isolated mistakes.

Overall, the pattern is of a capable but unreliable agent for production-critical use: it can fully resolve many incidents end-to-end, yet exhibits enough missed detections and incomplete remediations to limit trust in its consistency. The primary quality gap is not in basic understanding of network faults, but in robust, repeatable closure from diagnosis through verified recovery across all scenario variants.

dimensions.title: Response & Reasoning Quality
dimensions.body:
Across the 80 network_fault runs, the agent consistently achieved at least adequate diagnostic reasoning, with no instances of weak performance reported by any judge. The baseline quality is solid and dependable, and strong reasoning appears regularly across all network fault subtypes (pod-dns-error, pod-network-corruption, pod-network-loss, and pod-network-rate-limit), indicating broadly applicable competence rather than narrow specialization.

Variation is primarily between adequate and strong responses, with numerous runs highlighted for particularly strong reasoning and no clear negative outliers. The main improvement opportunity lies in making the strong-level reasoning more consistent rather than correcting systemic weaknesses. Overall, the evidence supports an assessment of generally strong reasoning quality that reliably meets adequacy and frequently exceeds it.

dimensions.title: Security Compliance
dimensions.body:
Across all 80 runs in the network_fault category, the agent consistently operated strictly within the authorized namespace scope, with no evidence of boundary overreach, privilege escalation, unauthorized resource access, or policy deviations. The run narratives are uniform and show no outliers or exceptions, indicating stable, repeatable adherence to the defined security constraints for this fault type.

This strong consistency suggests a robust and reliably enforced security posture within the evaluated dimension (namespace and access control behavior) for network_fault scenarios. While the assessment is limited to the logged aspects of security, the observed behavior across all runs supports a conclusion of strong compliance and low variance in security-related actions.

dimensions.title: RAI Compliance
dimensions.body:
Across all 80 runs in the network_fault category, the agent consistently produced technically appropriate and scenario-specific diagnostic outputs for a variety of network issues, including pod DNS errors, network corruption, network loss, and network rate limiting. Each network_fault subtype appears multiple times, and in every case the judges report that the agent’s response was suitable for the required diagnosis, indicating broad and reliable coverage of the category.

None of the judges identified outliers, failures, or signs of unsafe or incomplete behavior. The uniformly positive assessments across all runs suggest that the agent’s performance on network_fault diagnosis is stable, robust, and consistently high-quality, with no observed degradation or inconsistency over the evaluated runs.

dimensions.title: Agent Summary
dimensions.body:
Across 80 runs in the resource_fault category, the agent generally demonstrates solid diagnostic capability for resource-related issues (disk-fill, pod-autoscaler problems, pod-cpu-hog, and pod-memory-hog), with successful identification being more common than outright failures. However, performance is uneven across fault types: disk-fill and autoscaler issues tend to be diagnosed more reliably, while pod-cpu-hog (and to a lesser extent pod-memory-hog) show more frequent diagnostic misses and clusters of complete failures, indicating specific weak spots rather than a universal detection problem.

The most consistent weakness is remediation: many runs explicitly report that remediation was not completed even after a correct diagnosis, and only a subset of cases achieve fully applied and verified fixes. These repeated incomplete remediations, combined with non-trivial clusters of diagnostic failures, suggest that while the agent adds value as a diagnostic assistant and can sometimes carry through to full resolution, its behavior is not robust or consistent enough for high-criticality autonomous use without human oversight.

dimensions.title: Response & Reasoning Quality
dimensions.body:
Across the 80 resource_fault runs (disk-fill, pod-autoscaler, pod-cpu-hog, and pod-memory-hog), all three judges report that the agent’s reasoning quality is consistently at least adequate, with a clear majority of runs exhibiting strong reasoning. Performance is stable across fault types, indicating that the agent’s diagnostic approach generalizes well rather than being tailored to a single scenario. Variability is described as shifts between strong and adequate depth or clarity, not between success and failure.

None of the judges observed weak reasoning or notable negative outliers; the floor of performance is consistently adequate, and strong reasoning recurs frequently across the run sequence and fault types. Taken together, this supports a characterization of the agent’s overall response and reasoning quality in resource_fault scenarios as reliably high, with occasional but non-problematic dips from strong to adequate explanations.

dimensions.title: Security Compliance
dimensions.body:
Across all 80 runs in the resource_fault category, the agent consistently operated strictly within the authorized namespace scope, with no evidence of boundary violations, privilege escalation, or cross-namespace actions. All three judges report fully uniform narratives indicating that every operation remained within permitted security boundaries and that no deviations or negative findings were observed.

This uniformity across runs and evaluators suggests robust, repeatable adherence to namespace-based security constraints under the tested conditions. While the evaluations primarily focus on namespace scope rather than broader security dimensions, the available evidence supports a conclusion of strong and reliably enforced compliance for the security_compliance_summary metric in this fault category.

dimensions.title: RAI Compliance
dimensions.body:
Across all 80 runs in the resource_fault category, the agent consistently produced technically appropriate and scenario-specific diagnoses for resource-related issues, including disk-fill, pod-autoscaler problems, pod CPU hogs, and pod memory hogs. Each fault subtype appears multiple times and is handled correctly, with no reports of misdiagnosis, omissions, or degraded performance over time.

The evaluations describe a uniformly positive pattern with no notable outliers or failures, indicating both broad coverage of resource fault types and stable, repeatable behavior across runs. Overall, the agent demonstrates strong reliability and robustness in diagnosing resource_fault scenarios, with no evidence of scenario-specific weaknesses or regressions.

sections.title: Known Limitations
sections.intro: Known limitations are surfaced through analysis across all certification runs by the LLM Council. Each limitation is ranked by severity (High / Medium / Low) and shows how frequently it occurred. Items appear highest-severity first.

content.body: Responsible AI (RAI) compliance is incomplete in application_fault scenarios, with rai_compliance_rate = 0.8 (8/10 runs, 80%), meaning in 2/10 runs (20%) at least one RAI dimension (e.g., safety, transparency, or adherence to evaluation constraints) was not fully satisfied. This occurs despite perfect detection and mitigation for application faults (detection rate 100%, mitigation rate 100%, false negatives 0%, false positives 0%), indicating that behavioral issues are confined to policy adherence rather than technical fault handling.

End-to-end fault mitigation is unreliable for network_fault scenarios: only 39/80 runs (48.75%) reach successful mitigation (fault_mitigation_success_rate = 0.4875), while 41/80 runs (51.25%) fail to complete remediation, even though detection is perfect (detection rate 100%, false negatives 0%, false positives 0%). This indicates that more than half of network incidents stall after diagnosis or partial remediation, leaving faults unresolved in 51.25% of cases.
content.frequency: 39/80 runs (48.75%) successfully mitigated; 41/80 runs (51.25%) not mitigated

content.body: Remediation gaps are concentrated in specific network_fault subtypes, particularly pod-network-loss and pod-network-rate-limit, within the 41/80 network runs (51.25%) that do not reach successful mitigation. While overall network detection is 100% with 0% false negatives and 0% false positives, the agent often stops after diagnosis or fails to complete effective remediation for these subtypes, creating recurring weaknesses that drive the low overall network mitigation rate of 49% (39/80 runs).
content.frequency: Mitigation failures concentrated in specific subtypes within 41/80 unsuccessful runs (51.25%)

content.body: Resource_fault remediation is incomplete or unsuccessful in a large fraction of runs: fault_mitigation_success_rate is 0.5375, meaning only 37/80 runs (46.25%) are successfully mitigated and 43/80 runs (53.75%) fail to reach verified recovery. This occurs despite perfect detection (detection rate 100%, false negatives 0%, false positives 0%), showing that the agent frequently stops at diagnosis or applies partial fixes without fully validating recovery in over half of resource incidents.

Diagnostic and remediation performance for resource_faults is uneven across subtypes: disk-fill and pod-autoscaler scenarios are handled more reliably, while pod-cpu-hog and pod-memory-hog show more frequent diagnostic misses and clusters of complete failures. Given the overall resource mitigation rate of 54% (approximately 37/80 runs) and 43/80 runs (53.75%) failing to reach successful mitigation despite 100% detection, these subtype-specific weaknesses indicate that failures are not evenly distributed but concentrated in CPU and memory hog scenarios.
content.frequency: Subtype-specific failures within the 43/80 unsuccessful mitigation runs (53.75%)

content.body: Hallucinations are present in application_fault handling at a non-trivial but low level: hallucination detection is triggered in all 10/10 runs (100%), with a hallucination_score mean of 0.137 and max of 0.23. This indicates that while overall reasoning quality is strong (reasoning score mean 7.94/10), the agent occasionally introduces unsupported or extraneous assertions in every run, requiring continued monitoring even though severity per run is modest.
content.frequency: Hallucinations detected in 10/10 runs (100%) with low scores (mean 0.137, max 0.23)

content.body: Detection and mitigation latencies for application_faults are relatively high and variable: time_to_detect has mean 47.0s, median 51.8s, std dev 27.0s, and p95 88.4s (range 5.0–88.4s), while time_to_mitigate has mean 206.6s, median 177.4s, std dev 85.2s, and p95 364.5s (range 95.7–364.5s). Although all 10/10 application runs (100%) are successfully mitigated, these timings suggest the agent is not optimized for fast response in this category, which may be problematic for latency-sensitive environments.
content.frequency: 10/10 runs (100%) affected by relatively high detection and mitigation times

content.body: Action correctness for application_faults is imperfect: the mean action correctness score is 0.82 (median 0.835, std dev 0.1136) across 10/10 runs, implying that in roughly 1–2 of 10 runs (10–20%) the chosen actions are partially suboptimal or require correction. Despite a 100% mitigation rate, this indicates that the agent’s action sequences are not consistently optimal and may include unnecessary or inefficient steps.
content.frequency: Action correctness mean 0.82 across 10/10 runs; roughly 1–2 of 10 runs partially suboptimal

content.body: Time to mitigate network_faults is relatively high and variable across all 80/80 runs: time_to_mitigate has mean 300.5s, median 295.7s, std dev 119.6s, p95 564.1s, and max 585.9s (min 77.3s). Combined with a mean time_to_detect of 144.4s (median 145.2s, std dev 75.0s, p95 270.0s), these values indicate inconsistent and sometimes slow remediation workflows that may be unsuitable for latency-sensitive production incidents, especially given that only 39/80 runs (49%) reach successful mitigation.
content.frequency: 80/80 runs (100%) exhibit relatively high and variable mitigation times

content.body: Action correctness during network_fault handling is imperfect, with a mean score of 0.7689 (median 0.765, std dev 0.1066) across 80/80 runs. This implies that roughly 23% of actions taken are suboptimal or incorrect, contributing to inefficient troubleshooting paths and likely interacting with the low mitigation rate of 49% (39/80 runs) and high mitigation latencies (mean 300.5s, p95 564.1s).
content.frequency: Action correctness mean 0.7689 across 80/80 runs; ~23% of actions suboptimal

sections.title: Recommendations
sections.intro: Recommendations come from the LLM Council based on observed agent behavior, performance gaps, and compliance findings. Each one is prioritized by urgency (Critical / High / Medium / Low) and tied to the fault category where it was observed.

content.body: Redesign and enforce a mandatory end-to-end remediation workflow across all fault categories to close the current mitigation gaps (network fault_mitigation_success_rate = 0.4875, 39/80 runs; resource = 0.5375, 43/80 runs) and ensure consistent completion and verification. The workflow should: (1) require a structured sequence of diagnosis → targeted fix → explicit post-mitigation verification (e.g., connectivity/health checks, key metrics back to baseline, policy conformance) before incident closure; (2) gate success on meeting pre-defined criteria (e.g., services reachable, pods healthy, resource utilization normalized, rate limits cleared); and (3) include fallback and retry strategies when initial actions fail. This directly addresses L2 and L4 by turning partial or abandoned remediations into fully verified resolutions in both network and resource scenarios, while preserving the already strong 100% detection rate across categories.

Strengthen Responsible AI (RAI) compliance controls end-to-end to close the observed compliance gaps (application rai_compliance_rate = 0.8, 8/10 runs; network = 0.8375, 67/80 runs; resource = 0.7875, 63/80 runs), aiming to approach 100% compliance without degrading mitigation performance. Implement explicit pre- and post-action policy validations in the decision pipeline (e.g., checks for adherence to evaluation constraints, transparency in actions taken, and governance rules), log rationales for high-impact changes, and enforce guardrails that block or modify actions that risk non-compliance. Add targeted tests for the 2/10 non-compliant application runs and the 13/80 non-compliant network runs highlighted in L1, L11, and L18-equivalent resource gaps, ensuring that previously violating patterns are detected and prevented in future runs.

Optimize detection and mitigation workflows across all categories to reduce high and variable time_to_detect and time_to_mitigate, especially where means and p95 values are elevated (network TTD mean 144.4s, p95 270.0s; resource TTD mean 103.9s, p95 158.3s; network TTM mean 300.5s, p95 564.1s; resource TTM mean 235.9s, p95 357.2s; application TTM mean 206.6s, p95 364.5s). Streamline log/metric queries, cache common diagnostic paths, and parallelize safe verification steps to bring TTD/TTM closer to their minimums (5.0–111.8s) and reduce p95 outliers. Introduce time-bound decision policies (e.g., escalate or switch strategy if mitigation exceeds a defined SLA threshold) to address L7, L9, and L13 while preserving the current 100% detection rate and strong mitigation where it already exists (application 100% mitigation).

Improve action selection robustness and correctness across all fault types to raise action correctness scores (application mean 0.82; network 0.7689; resource 0.7554) and reduce the spread (std dev ≈0.10–0.11) noted in L8 and L10. Implement an action validation layer that checks proposed actions against expected outcomes and known-safe patterns (e.g., configuration changes aligned with diagnoses and policies), add guardrails such as validation checklists and optional dry-run/simulation steps, and introduce feedback loops that compare planned actions against historically successful remediations. For each diagnosed fault, ensure there is a logged, matching remediation and verification step, and trigger a self-correction loop when diagnosis is not followed by a corresponding fix, thereby converting partially correct or incomplete action sequences into fully aligned remediations.

Develop and integrate subtype- or pattern-specific remediation playbooks for recurring fault patterns across categories, then automate their selection once corresponding diagnostic signatures are detected. For example, where current data show concentrated failures in certain network and resource sub-patterns (L3 and L5) and overall mitigation rates below 0.55, define canonical command sequences, configuration checks, rollback strategies, and verification steps for each pattern. Embed these playbooks into the agent’s decision logic so that, after diagnosis, the agent reliably executes the appropriate remediation sequence instead of stopping early or improvising, thereby improving fault_mitigation_success_rate beyond the current 0.4875 (network) and 0.5375 (resource) while maintaining the 100% detection rate.

Standardize reasoning and explanation templates across all categories to reduce variability in reasoning quality (overall normalized reasoning score 0.79) and to address uneven performance highlighted in L5 and L8–L10. Introduce structured templates with explicit sections for hypothesis, evidence (with references to specific logs/metrics), action plan, and verification results, and define consistency-focused diagnostic trees and decision matrices for common patterns. This will help ensure that the agent consistently applies its strongest reasoning patterns, reduces divergence between adequate and strong responses, and makes it easier to audit decisions for RAI and security compliance.

Tighten hallucination control and grounding mechanisms across all categories, where hallucination detection is triggered in 100% of runs and the overall normalized hallucination score is 0.98, to minimize unsupported statements while preserving useful explanatory detail (L6). Require evidence-backed reasoning for key diagnostic and remediation claims, enforce alignment with observed telemetry and logs, and add post-generation filters that down-rank or correct content associated with higher hallucination scores. Explicitly label hypotheses as such when evidence is incomplete, and leverage the existing 1.0 hallucination detection_rate to automatically flag speculative content for suppression or revision before final output.

Enhance security compliance instrumentation and controls across all categories to improve the overall normalized security score (0.89) and close the specific gap in network scenarios (security_compliance_rate = 0.7875, 63/80 runs compliant, L12). Add automated checks that ensure all actions remain within authorized namespaces and access scopes, validate that proposed changes respect cluster and organizational policies, and flag any ambiguous or borderline operations for review. Integrate these checks into the same pre-/post-action validation pipeline used for RAI, and log security rationales alongside RAI rationales so that non-compliant patterns can be systematically identified and eliminated without regressing mitigation success.
